aliases: Padang rice with sour spicy fish curry

english: Padang rice with sour spicy fish curry

region_of_origin: Padang, West Sumatra, Indonesia; widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Nasi Padang; 'rice dish' (FOODON_00002539) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Nasi Padang; A000E (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Nasi Padang or equivalent composite Minangkabau rice dish.

preparation_method: slow-cooked in coconut milk and spices
main_ingredients: fish (typically mackerel or stingray) in assam pedas sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nasi Padang (Assam Pedas) is approximately 450 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice (roughly 200–250 g) accompanied by a portion of assam pedas fish curry and one or two additional side dishes such as a vegetable curry or sambal.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Nasi Padang and composite rice dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) is the primary source for Nasi Padang composite dish values. The specific assam pedas variant is adapted from the closest HPB Nasi Padang entry. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for analogous Southeast Asian composite rice dishes where HPB does not report individual micronutrients. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.5 g/100 g based on known coconut-milk-heavy Minangkabau dish composition per FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — composite rice-based meals with protein and fat side dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Padang Assam Pedas in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy with published values for white rice-based composite meals containing protein-rich and fat-rich side dishes (coconut milk curries, fish). White rice alone has a GI of approximately 72–89; the addition of high-fat coconut-milk curries, fish protein, and dietary fibre from vegetables is well-documented to attenuate the glycemic response, yielding an estimated composite GI of approximately 55–65. A midpoint of 60 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (60 × 83.25) / 100 ≈ 49.9 g (available carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 18.5 × 4.5 = 83.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (380 mg/100 g; approximately 1,710 mg per 450 g serving) from multiple seasoned dishes, sambals, and belacan (shrimp paste)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Nasi Padang (Assam Pedas) to occasional consumption, select lower-sodium side dishes, avoid additional sambal or belacan, and keep the serving size to no more than 300 g to stay within a 1,500 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (approximately 50 g GL) driven by a large portion of refined white rice, partially offset by protein and fat from coconut-milk curries and fish
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, increase the proportion of vegetable-based and lean-protein side dishes, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the estimated glycemic load of a standard serving is high despite a medium GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (approximately 3.5 g/100 g) from heavy use of coconut milk in curries, combined with high sodium, raises both LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Nasi Padang (Assam Pedas) to occasional consumption, choose grilled or steamed fish and vegetable sides over coconut-milk-heavy curries, and keep total serving size small to manage saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, galangal, chili) in the assam pedas and curry components may offer modest benefit, but high saturated fat and sodium can be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile of assam pedas, but should limit coconut-milk-heavy sides and monitor sodium intake to avoid exacerbating inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Padang (Assam Pedas) is a Minangkabau-style rice platter originating from Padang, West Sumatra, Indonesia, and widely popular across Malaysia and Singapore. The dish centres on a generous mound of steamed white rice served alongside multiple pre-cooked side dishes, with the defining component being assam pedas — a sour and fiery fish curry made with tamarind (assam), fresh chili, galangal, lemongrass, turmeric, shallots, garlic, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste), often garnished with torch ginger flower (bunga kantan). The overall flavour profile is intensely savoury, sour, spicy, and aromatic, with a rich, slightly oily mouthfeel from coconut milk-based curries. The texture contrasts soft, sticky rice with tender braised or slow-cooked fish and vegetables. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 787 kcal, 29 g protein, 38 g fat (including approximately 16 g saturated fat from coconut milk), and 83 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is very high at approximately 1,710 mg per serving, making it a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per serving is high (approximately 50), driven by the large white rice portion, though the medium GI of approximately 60 reflects partial attenuation by protein, fat, and fibre from the side dishes. The dish is halal and dairy-free. It is best consumed as an occasional meal rather than a daily staple for individuals managing cardiometabolic conditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Nasi Padang?
What is the glycemic index of Nasi Padang?
Is Nasi Padang high in sodium?
What are the side dishes in Nasi Padang?
What does assam pedas taste like?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo for Nasi Padang composite dishes (adapted for the assam pedas variant). Micronutrients are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for analogous Southeast Asian composite rice dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for coconut-milk-heavy Minangkabau dish composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for composite white rice meals with protein and fat side dishes. No exact published nutrient analysis for Nasi Padang Assam Pedas as a single composite dish was found in any single approved source; therefore confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite Minangkabau rice dish are not individually reported in HPB Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16; these values are genuinely unavailable in any approved source consulted. saturated_fat_g (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for coconut-milk-based Southeast Asian composite dishes and is not directly measured for this specific dish. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Nasi Padang Assam Pedas exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. The input source 'Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition' (https://apjcn.nhri.org.tw) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; all nutrient values are re-anchored to HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin A is reported as mcg RAE (retinol activity equivalents) per USDA convention; the input value of 35 mcg has been retained as mcg RAE. Folate is reported as mcg DFE; the input value of 32 mcg has been retained as mcg DFE. This dish is popular in Singapore hawker centres and Malaysian Nasi Padang restaurants; the assam pedas variant specifically features a tamarind-based sour-spicy fish curry as the primary side dish.

All values are non-zero and plausible for a composite rice dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (60), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (49.9), and gi_source all present. GI sourced by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database for composite white rice meals; estimation basis fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002539 ('rice dish') used as broader anchor with gap note. FoodEx2 A000E ('rice-based composite dish') used as broader anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible broader-class anchors with honest documentation.
